evidence_quotes.quote: placing artificial end dates to videogames.
your account can be permanently locked up thus barring you from accessing any and all of your games

paragraphs.text: Dear EU Commissioner, As a European resident myself, I've taken the time from my day in order to write this short message relating to the proposed Digital Fairness Act. In my opinion this Act should not ignore the biggest industry in entertainment today, and the number one type of media teenagers and young adults spend their money on, that being videogames. Videogames have been sold through digital/online marketplaces for more than two decades by now. However, unlike physical goods, videogames are distributed as digital files over the internet that you have to download from a specific type of software which acts as a storefront, on which you often have to create an account, and install it on a specific device that you own, be it a dedicated videogame console or a device designed for more of a general use such as a personal computer or a smartphone device. Because of the need of an internet connection and a dedicated account to use the digital storefront there's an often untold "expiration date" placed on your ability to purchase games and even the ability to play them. Stores are being shut down as consoles end their lifecycles or the companies who create them cease to exist. Usually however, if you keep a copy of the games you bought on your device then you can play them for as long as your device is functional, which is what's expected when you make a purchase like this. This brings me to the main topic I want to discuss. Recently there's been a rise to a new trend in the gaming industry, that being placing artificial end dates to videogames. Companies such as Ubisoft have been forcing various games, such as "The Crew" into an "always online" model. That means you are required to connect to their servers if you want to simply access and play your game. This causes the problem that eventually and without any prior notice the servers of the game can be terminated thus rendering the game inaccessible. This is often done by these companies in order to "force" consumers to purchase a newer version of their game, that can be more expensive, if they want to continue playing. This is, in my opinion, an anti-consumer practice that goes against the right of the consumer to use the product they bought however they want and for as long as they want. Another way a consumer might lose their purchased games is by being punished by a moderation team if they are deemed to be acting inappropriately. Companies such as Electronic Arts operate their own personal storefronts as well as online games. If your associated account is deemed to be misbehaving on an online game of theirs then your account can be permanently locked up thus barring you from accessing any and all of your games including other games that are always offline and are not associated with the one you were banned from. While being banned from a game for breaking the rules of is fair, it is absolutely outrageous to have all of your purchased games being taken away forever. Thank you for considering what I had to tell you, I can only hope that the EU will once again stand by her citizens and protect them from anti-consumer practices.
